title: Bossa nova

infobox.value: Late 1950s, [South Zone](/wiki/South_Zone_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "South Zone (Rio de Janeiro)") of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil

(Portuguese pronunciation: [[ˈbɔsɐ ˈnɔvɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Br-BossaNova.ogg "File:Br-BossaNova.ogg")) is a relaxed style of [samba](/wiki/Samba "Samba")[[nb 1]](#cite_note-2) developed in the late 1950s and early 1960s in [Rio de Janeiro, Brazil](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro,_Brazil "Rio de Janeiro, Brazil").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEBC2018-3) It is mainly characterized by a calm [syncopated](/wiki/Syncopation "Syncopation") rhythm with chords and fingerstyle mimicking the beat of a samba groove, as if it was a simplification and stylization on the guitar of the rhythm produced by a [samba school](/wiki/Samba_school "Samba school") band. Another defining characteristic of the style is the use of unconventional chords in some cases with complex progressions and "ambiguous" harmonies.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERatliff2019-4)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201546-5) A common misconception is that these complex chords and harmonies were derived from [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), but samba guitar players have been using similar [arrangement](/wiki/Arrangement "Arrangement") structures since the early 1920s, indicating a case of [parallel evolution](/wiki/Parallel_evolution "Parallel evolution") of styles rather than a simple transference from jazz to bossa nova.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201548-6)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcia2019-7) Nevertheless, bossa nova was influenced by jazz, both in the harmonies used and also by the instrumentation of songs, and today many bossa nova songs are considered [jazz standards](/wiki/Jazz_standard "Jazz standard"). The popularity of bossa nova has helped to renew samba and contributed to the modernization of [Brazilian music](/wiki/Music_of_Brazil "Music of Brazil") in general.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Style of Brazilian music

For other uses, see [Bossa nova (disambiguation)](/wiki/Bossa_nova_(disambiguation) "Bossa nova (disambiguation)").

"Bossa" redirects here. For other uses, see [Bossa (disambiguation)](/wiki/Bossa_(disambiguation) "Bossa (disambiguation)").

| Bossa nova | |
| --- | --- |
| [Vinicius de Moraes](/wiki/Vinicius_de_Moraes "Vinicius de Moraes") and [Baden Powell](/wiki/Baden_Powell_(guitarist) "Baden Powell (guitarist)") at the Teatro da Praia | |
| Stylistic origins | * [Samba](/wiki/Samba "Samba") * [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") * [choro](/wiki/Choro "Choro") * [classical](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") |
| Cultural origins | Late 1950s, [South Zone](/wiki/South_Zone_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "South Zone (Rio de Janeiro)") of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil |
| Derivative forms | * [Samba-jazz](/wiki/Samba-jazz "Samba-jazz") * [sambalanço](/wiki/Sambalan%C3%A7o "Sambalanço") * [sambass](/wiki/Sambass "Sambass") |

**Bossa nova** (Portuguese pronunciation: [[ˈbɔsɐ ˈnɔvɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Br-BossaNova.ogg "File:Br-BossaNova.ogg")) is a relaxed style of [samba](/wiki/Samba "Samba")[[nb 1]](#cite_note-2) developed in the late 1950s and early 1960s in [Rio de Janeiro, Brazil](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro,_Brazil "Rio de Janeiro, Brazil").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEBC2018-3) It is mainly characterized by a calm [syncopated](/wiki/Syncopation "Syncopation") rhythm with chords and fingerstyle mimicking the beat of a samba groove, as if it was a simplification and stylization on the guitar of the rhythm produced by a [samba school](/wiki/Samba_school "Samba school") band. Another defining characteristic of the style is the use of unconventional chords in some cases with complex progressions and "ambiguous" harmonies.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERatliff2019-4)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201546-5) A common misconception is that these complex chords and harmonies were derived from [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), but samba guitar players have been using similar [arrangement](/wiki/Arrangement "Arrangement") structures since the early 1920s, indicating a case of [parallel evolution](/wiki/Parallel_evolution "Parallel evolution") of styles rather than a simple transference from jazz to bossa nova.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201548-6)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcia2019-7) Nevertheless, bossa nova was influenced by jazz, both in the harmonies used and also by the instrumentation of songs, and today many bossa nova songs are considered [jazz standards](/wiki/Jazz_standard "Jazz standard"). The popularity of bossa nova has helped to renew samba and contributed to the modernization of [Brazilian music](/wiki/Music_of_Brazil "Music of Brazil") in general.

One of the major innovations of bossa nova was the way to synthesize the rhythm of samba on the [classical guitar](/wiki/Classical_guitar "Classical guitar").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEBC2018-3)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcia2019-7) According to musicologist [Gilberto Mendes](/wiki/Gilberto_Mendes "Gilberto Mendes"), the bossa nova was one of the "three rhythmic phases of samba", in which the "bossa beat" had been extracted by [João Gilberto](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Gilberto "João Gilberto") from the traditional samba.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201548-6) The synthesis performed by [Gilberto](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Gilberto "João Gilberto")'s guitar was a reduction of the "[batucada](/wiki/Batucada "Batucada")" of samba, a stylization produced from one of the percussion instruments: the thumb stylized a [surdo](/wiki/Surdo "Surdo"); the index, middle and ring fingers phrased like a tamborim.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcia2019-7) In line with this thesis, musicians such as [Baden Powell](/wiki/Baden_Powell_(guitarist) "Baden Powell (guitarist)"), [Roberto Menescal](/wiki/Roberto_Menescal "Roberto Menescal"), and [Ronaldo Bôscoli](/wiki/Ronaldo_B%C3%B4scoli "Ronaldo Bôscoli") also understand the bossa nova beat as being extracted from the [tamborim](/wiki/Tamborim "Tamborim") play in the [bateria](/wiki/Bateria "Bateria").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarcia199921-8)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bossa_nova&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Bossa_nova_dance_pattern.png/500px-Bossa_nova_dance_pattern.png)](/wiki/File:Bossa_nova_dance_pattern.png)

Bossa nova [rhythm](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm")[[8]](#cite_note-9)

In Brazil, the word *bossa* is old-fashioned slang for something done with particular charm, natural flair or innate ability. As early as 1932, [Noel Rosa](/wiki/Noel_Rosa "Noel Rosa") used the word in a samba:

> *O samba, a prontidão e outras bossas são nossas coisas, são coisas nossas.*  
> (Samba, readiness and other *bossas* are our things, are things from us.)

The phrase *bossa nova*, translated literally, means "new trend" or "new wave" in Portuguese.[[9]](#cite_note-10) The exact origin of the term *bossa nova* remained unclear for many decades, according to some authors. Within the artistic beach culture of the late 1950s in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), the term *bossa* was used to refer to any new "trend" or "fashionable wave". In his book *Bossa Nova*, Brazilian author [Ruy Castro](/wiki/Ruy_Castro "Ruy Castro") asserts that *bossa* was already in use in the 1950s by musicians as a word to characterize someone's knack for playing or singing idiosyncratically.[[10]](#cite_note-Castro/Bossa-Nova-11)

Castro claims that the term *bossa nova* might have first been used in public for a concert given in 1957 by the Grupo Universitário Hebraico do Brasil ('Hebrew University Group of Brazil'). The authorship of the term *bossa nova* is attributed to the young journalist Moyses Fuks, who was promoting the event.[[11]](#cite_note-12) That group consisted of [Sylvia Telles](/wiki/Sylvia_Telles "Sylvia Telles"), [Carlos Lyra](/wiki/Carlos_Lyra "Carlos Lyra"), [Nara Leão](/wiki/Nara_Le%C3%A3o "Nara Leão"), [Luiz Eça](/wiki/Luiz_E%C3%A7a "Luiz Eça"), [Roberto Menescal](/wiki/Roberto_Menescal "Roberto Menescal"), and others. Mr Fuks's description, fully supported by most of the bossa nova members, simply read *"HOJE. SYLVIA TELLES E UM GRUPO BOSSA NOVA"* ("Today. Sylvia Telles and a 'Bossa Nova' group"), since Sylvia Telles was the most famous musician in the group at that time.

In 1959, Nara Leão also participated in more than one embryonic display of bossa nova. These include the 1st Festival de Samba Session, conducted by the student union of [Pontifícia Universidade Católica](/wiki/Pontif%C3%ADcia_Universidade_Cat%C3%B3lica "Pontifícia Universidade Católica"). This session was chaired by [Carlos Diegues](/wiki/Carlos_Diegues "Carlos Diegues") (later a prominent [Cinema Novo](/wiki/Cinema_Novo "Cinema Novo") [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director")), a law student whom Leão ultimately married.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bossa_nova&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The nightclubs of [Beco das Garrafas](/w/index.php?title=Beco_das_Garrafas&action=edit&redlink=1 "Beco das Garrafas (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beco_das_Garrafas "pt:Beco das Garrafas")] (Alley of the Bottles), a small dead-end alley in Copacabana, is considered a historical cradle of bossa nova.[[13]](#cite_note-musicorigins/Bottles-14)[[14]](#cite_note-rio-aqui/garrafas-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-tocaevents/nova-then-17)[[17]](#cite_note-connectbrazil/Carnegie-18)[[18]](#cite_note-postoseis/inicio-da-bossa-19)[[19]](#cite_note-riotur/garrafas-20)[[10]](#cite_note-Castro/Bossa-Nova-11)

In 1959, the soundtrack to the film *[Black Orpheus](/wiki/Black_Orpheus "Black Orpheus")* (Orfeu Negro) was released, which included the future [Manhã de Carnaval](/wiki/Manh%C3%A3_de_Carnaval "Manhã de Carnaval"), "The Morning of the Carnival". The style emerged at the time when [samba-canção](/wiki/Samba-can%C3%A7%C3%A3o "Samba-canção")[[nb 2]](#cite_note-22) was the dominant rhythm in the Brazilian music scene.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeveriano2009273-23)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatos2015130-24) Its first appearance was on the album *[Canção do Amor Demais](/wiki/Can%C3%A7%C3%A3o_do_Amor_Demais "Canção do Amor Demais")*, in which the singer [Elizeth Cardoso](/wiki/Elizeth_Cardoso "Elizeth Cardoso") recorded two compositions by the duo [Antônio Carlos Jobim](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_Jobim "Antônio Carlos Jobim") and [Vinicius de Moraes](/wiki/Vinicius_de_Moraes "Vinicius de Moraes"), "Outra Vez" and "[Chega de Saudade](/wiki/Chega_de_Saudade "Chega de Saudade")", which were accompanied by João Gilberto's guitar. It was the first time that the Bahian musician presented the beat of his guitar that would become characteristic of the style.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEBC2018-3) By accompanying Cardoso's voice, Gilberto innovated in the way of pacing the rhythm, accentuating the weak times, to carry out a synthesis of the beat of samba to guitar.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEBC2018-3)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro2018-25)

In 1959, João Gilberto's bossa album was released, containing the tracks "Chega de Saudade", "Bim Bom" and "Desafinado" which entered the jazz repertory.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro2018-25) Considered the landmark of the birth of bossa nova,[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEBC2018-3)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastro2018-25) it also featured Gilberto's innovative way of singing samba, which was inspired by [Dorival Caymmi](/wiki/Dorival_Caymmi "Dorival Caymmi").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMachado201136-26)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaymmi2001377-27) With the LP *[Chega de Saudade](/wiki/Chega_de_Saudade_(album) "Chega de Saudade (album)")*, released in 1959, Gilberto consolidated the bossa nova as a new style of playing samba.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEBC2018-3)[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERatliff2019-4) His innovative way of playing and singing samba, combined with the harmonies of Antônio Carlos Jobim and the lyrics of Vinicius de Moraes, found immediate resonance among musicians who were looking for new approaches to samba in Rio de Janeiro,[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEBC2018-3)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977106–107-28) many of whom were influenced by American [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilva2017133-137-29)

On 21 November 1962, the [Consulate-General of Brazil](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_Brazil#Americas "List of diplomatic missions of Brazil") presented *Bossa Nova* at [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall").[[17]](#cite_note-connectbrazil/Carnegie-18)[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-jazzwax/new-beat-1962-31)[[30]](#cite_note-jazz.fm/rage-60s-america-32)[[31]](#cite_note-carloslyra/bio2-33)

In 1964, João Gilberto, Stan Getz and [Jobim](/wiki/Tom_Jobim "Tom Jobim") released the [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards")-winning album *[Getz/Gilberto](/wiki/Getz/Gilberto "Getz/Gilberto")*. Bossa Nova emerged an artistic movement around Gilberto and other professional artists such as Jobim, Moraes and [Baden Powell](/wiki/Baden_Powell_(guitarist) "Baden Powell (guitarist)"), among others, which attracted young amateur musicians from the [South Zone of Rio](/wiki/South_Zone_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "South Zone (Rio de Janeiro)") – such as [Marcos Valle](/wiki/Marcos_Valle "Marcos Valle"), [Carlos Lyra](/wiki/Carlos_Lyra "Carlos Lyra"), [Roberto Menescal](/wiki/Roberto_Menescal "Roberto Menescal"), Ronaldo Bôscoli and [Nara Leão](/wiki/Nara_Le%C3%A3o "Nara Leão") and Bahian [Astrud Gilberto](/wiki/Astrud_Gilberto "Astrud Gilberto").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcondes1977106–107-28)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELopesSimas201546–47-34)

[Jorge Ben](/wiki/Jorge_Ben "Jorge Ben") wrote "[Mas que Nada](/wiki/Mas_que_Nada "Mas que Nada")" in 1963, and [Sérgio Mendes](/wiki/S%C3%A9rgio_Mendes "Sérgio Mendes") & Brazil 66 gained a bossa rock hit "Mas que Nada" in 1966.[[33]](#cite_note-35) It was inducted into the [Latin Grammy Hall of Fame](/wiki/Latin_Grammy_Hall_of_Fame "Latin Grammy Hall of Fame"), and following the success of [Waters of March](/wiki/Waters_of_March "Waters of March") in the 1970s, the song was adapted for use in a series of advertisements for [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola") in the 1980s. These ended with the then current slogan "Coke Is It".[[34]](#cite_note-36)
[Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") and [Antônio Carlos Jobim](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_Jobim "Antônio Carlos Jobim") recorded [Sinatra & Company](/wiki/Francis_Albert_Sinatra_%26_Antonio_Carlos_Jobim "Francis Albert Sinatra & Antonio Carlos Jobim") in 1967.[[35]](#cite_note-Castro2012-37) The tracks were arranged and conducted by [Claus Ogerman](/wiki/Claus_Ogerman "Claus Ogerman"), accompanied by a studio orchestra. Along with Jobim's original compositions. In the 1960s and 1970s, US jazz artists such as Stan Getz, Hank Mobley, Dave Brubeck, Zoot Sims, Paul Winter, and [Quincy Jones](/wiki/Quincy_Jones "Quincy Jones") recorded bossa jazz albums.

Bossa nova continues to influence popular music around the world, from the 1960s to today. An example is the song "[Break on Through (To the Other Side)](/wiki/Break_On_Through_(To_the_Other_Side) "Break On Through (To the Other Side)")" by American rock band [The Doors](/wiki/The_Doors "The Doors"), especially the drum beat. Drummer [John Densmore](/wiki/John_Densmore "John Densmore") has stated that he was very influenced by the sounds of Brazil when coming up with the drum part for the song.[[36]](#cite_note-38) In the mid-1980s, New Bossa emerged in Europe, in the work of Nigerian singer [Sade Adu](/wiki/Sade_Adu "Sade Adu") and in groups such as [Matt Bianco](/wiki/Matt_Bianco "Matt Bianco") and [Style Council](/wiki/Style_Council "Style Council").[[37]](#cite_note-39) In 21st century, the song "Billie Bossa Nova" was released by [Billie Eilish](/wiki/Billie_Eilish "Billie Eilish") in 2021,[[38]](#cite_note-40) the Icelandic jazz pop singer [Laufey](/wiki/Laufey_(singer) "Laufey (singer)") released her hit song "[From the Start](/wiki/From_the_Start "From the Start")" in 2023, with its bossa nova–infused rhythm,[[39]](#cite_note-41) and success [BMF](/wiki/BMF_(song) "BMF (song)") by [SZA](/wiki/SZA "SZA") mixing with pop in 2025.[[40]](#cite_note-RS-Review-42)

## Instruments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bossa_nova&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Instruments")]

### Classical guitar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bossa_nova&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Classical guitar")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Jarkko_Toivonen_Jumo_Jazz_Club_1993.jpg/250px-Jarkko_Toivonen_Jumo_Jazz_Club_1993.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jarkko_Toivonen_Jumo_Jazz_Club_1993.jpg)

Jarkko Toivonen performing in 1993

Bossa nova is most commonly performed on the nylon-string [classical guitar](/wiki/Classical_guitar "Classical guitar"), played with the fingers rather than with a [pick](/wiki/Guitar_pick "Guitar pick"). Its purest form could be considered unaccompanied guitar with vocals, as created, pioneered, and exemplified by [João Gilberto](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Gilberto "João Gilberto"). Even in larger, jazz-influenced ensemble arrangements, a guitar is typically present to provide the foundational rhythm. Gilberto basically took one of the several rhythmic layers from a [samba ensemble](/wiki/Samba_ensemble "Samba ensemble"), specifically the [tamborim](/wiki/Tamborim "Tamborim"), and applied it to the picking hand. According to Brazilian musician Paulo Bittencourt, João Gilberto, known for his eccentricity and obsessed by the idea of finding a new way of playing the guitar, sometimes locked himself in the bathroom, where he played one and the same chord for many hours in a row.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Basic_bossa_nova_guitar_rhythm.PNG/250px-Basic_bossa_nova_guitar_rhythm.PNG)](/wiki/File:Basic_bossa_nova_guitar_rhythm.PNG)

A basic bossa nova guitar rhythm for a C major chord. Note the syncopation in the chord's rhythm and the sixth and ninth added to the C major for a richer sound.

### Drums and percussion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bossa_nova&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Drums and percussion")]

As in samba, the [surdo](/wiki/Surdo "Surdo") plays an [ostinato](/wiki/Ostinato "Ostinato") figure on the downbeat of beat one, the "ah" of beat one, the downbeat of beat two and the "ah" of beat two. The [clave](/wiki/Clave_(rhythm) "Clave (rhythm)") pattern sounds very similar to the two-three or three-two son clave of Cuban styles such as [mambo](/wiki/Mambo_(music) "Mambo (music)") but is dissimilar in that the "two" side of the clave is pushed by an eighth note. Also important in the percussion section for bossa nova is the [cabasa](/wiki/Cabasa "Cabasa"), which plays a steady sixteenth-note pattern. These parts are easily adaptable to the drum set, which makes bossa nova a rather popular Brazilian style for drummers.

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bossa_nova&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Structure")]

Certain other instrumentations and vocals are also part of the structure of bossa nova. These include:

### Bossa nova and samba

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bossa_nova&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bossa nova and samba")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Bossa-nova-rhythm1.svg/500px-Bossa-nova-rhythm1.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bossa-nova-rhythm1.svg)

Basic bossa nova accompaniment pattern ([play](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/c/c5/Bossa-nova-rhythm1.mid/Bossa-nova-rhythm1.mid.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Bossa-nova-rhythm1.mid "File:Bossa-nova-rhythm1.mid"))

Bossa nova has at its core a [rhythm](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm") based on [samba](/wiki/Samba "Samba"). Samba combines the rhythmic patterns and feel originating in afro-Brazilian slave communities. Samba's emphasis on the second [beat](/wiki/Beat_(music) "Beat (music)") carries through to bossa nova (to the degree that it is often notated in 2/4 time). However, unlike samba, bossa nova has no dance steps to accompany it.[[42]](#cite_note-44) When played on the guitar, in a simple one-bar pattern, the thumb plays the bass notes on 1 and 2, while the fingers pluck the chords in unison on the two eighth notes of beat one, followed by the second sixteenth note of beat two. Two-measure patterns usually contain a [syncopation](/wiki/Syncopation "Syncopation") into the second measure. Syncopation is a common feature of bossa nova, giving it its distinct "swaying" motion. While jazz music, which is typically swung, also contains syncopation, bossa nova is typically played without swing, contrasting with jazz. As bossa nova composer [Carlos Lyra](/wiki/Carlos_Lyra "Carlos Lyra") describes it in his song "Influência do Jazz", the samba rhythm moves "side to side" while jazz moves "front to back". There's also some evidence indicating a musical influence of blues in bossa nova, even thought this effect is not immediately recognized in the genre structure.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

### Vocals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bossa_nova&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Vocals")]

Aside from the guitar style, João Gilberto's other innovation was the projection of the singing voice. Prior to bossa nova, Brazilian singers employed brassy, almost operatic styles. Now, the characteristic nasal vocal production of bossa nova is a peculiar trait of the [caboclo](/wiki/Caboclo "Caboclo") folk tradition of northeastern Brazil.[[44]](#cite_note-46)[[45]](#cite_note-47)

### Themes and lyrics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bossa_nova&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Themes and lyrics")]

The lyrical themes found in bossa nova include women, love, longing, homesickness, and nature. Bossa Nova was often apolitical. The musical lyrics of the late 1950s depicted the easy life of middle to upper-class Brazilians, though the majority of the population was working class. In conjunction with political developments of the early 1960s (especially the 1964 military [coup d'état](/wiki/1964_Brazilian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1964 Brazilian coup d'état")), the popularity of bossa nova was eclipsed by [Música popular brasileira](/wiki/M%C3%BAsica_popular_brasileira "Música popular brasileira"), a musical genre that appeared around the mid-1960s, featuring lyrics that were more politically charged and focused on the working class struggle.

## Dance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bossa_nova&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Dance")]

**Bossa nova** was also a [fad dance](/wiki/Fad_dance "Fad dance") that corresponded to the music. It was introduced in the late 1950s and faded out in the mid-sixties.[[46]](#cite_note-48)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] Bossa nova music, with its soft, sophisticated vocal rhythms and improvisations, is well suited for listening but failed to become dance music despite heavy promotion in the 1960s. The style of basic dance steps suited the music well. It was danced on "soft" knees that allowed for sideways sways with hip motions and it could be danced both solo and in pairs. About ten various simple step patterns were published.

A variant of basic 8-beat pattern was: "step forward, tap, step back, step together, repeat from the opposite foot". A variation of this pattern was a kind of slow [samba](/wiki/Samba_(Brazilian_dance) "Samba (Brazilian dance)") walk, with "step together" above replaced by "replace". [Box steps](/wiki/Box_step "Box step") of [rhumba](/wiki/Rhumba "Rhumba") and [whisk](/wiki/Whisk_(ballroom_dance) "Whisk (ballroom dance)") steps of [nightclub two step](/wiki/Nightclub_two_step "Nightclub two step") could be fitted with bossa-nova styling. Embellishments included placing one arm onto one's own belly and waving another arm at waist level in the direction of the sway, possibly with a finger click.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
